coredata.dc:title: Tunable excitation-dependent-fluorescence of carbon dots: Fingerprint curves for super anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Ke
Qi, Rui
Lan, Shuai
Wang, Hongyang
Zheng, Xuefang
Liu, Chun
Jia, Dandan
Cao, Lei
Wang, Dongjun
coredata.dc:description: A novel concept of anti-counterfeiting is proposed, based on the special properties of excitation-dependent-fluorescence (EDF) and emission-dependent-excitation (EDE) of carbon dots (CDs) in polymer matrix. EDF and EDE behaviors can be controlled by adjusting the concentration of CDs or changing species of CDs and their corresponding polymer matrix. Meanwhile, multi-emission in EDF spectrum is observed, which is helpful to identification. Particularly, the curves in EDF or EDE spectrum as unique and sophisticated as human fingerprints are highly difficult to replicate, which promise practical applications of CDs in the field of anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Excitation-dependent fluorescence
Concentration-dependent fluorescence
Anti-Counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability test of CDs in polymer matrix
Multi-emission in EDF and EDE spectra of PEPA-CDs
EDF spectra and FL lifetime of DETA-CDs
Different systems of CDs in polymer matrix
Double-layer film of different kinds of CDs
Application example in anti-counterfeiting of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra of CDs: (a) (d) PEPA-CDs, (b) (e) DETA-CDs and (c) (f) HP-PAMAM-CDs.

FL and UV–vis absorption spectra of PEPA-CDs in the PVA film at different concentrations: (a), (c), (e) EDE and UV–vis absorption (dash line) spectra at 30 wt%, 50 wt% and 70 wt%, respectively; (b), (d), (f) EDF spectra at 30 wt%, 50 wt% and 70 wt%, respectively.
EDF spectra of PEPA-CDs in the PVA aqueous solution at different concentrations: (a) 30 wt%, (b) 50 wt% and (c) 70 wt%.
FL spectra of DETA-CDs in the UV curing polymer at 3 wt%, 5 wt%, 7 wt% and 9 wt%, respectively: (a–d) EDF spectra; (e) (f) Fluorescence decay curves measured at 400 nm and 435 nm, respectively.
EDE and UV–vis absorption (dash line) spectra of DETA-CDs in the UV curing polymer at (a) 3 wt%, (b) 5 wt%, (c) 7 wt% and (d) 9 wt%, respectively.

EDF spectra for different species of CDs in two kinds polymer matrix at the same concentration of 5 wt%: (a) DETA-CDs and (b) HP-PAMAM-CDs in the PVA film, (c) PEPA-CDs and (d) HP-PAMAM-CDs in the NC film.

Schematic image (left) and EDF spectra (right): (a) PEPA-CDs in the PVA film, (b) DETA-CDs in the NC film, and (c) double-layer sample of the two films. The concentration of all samples are kept at 9 wt%.

Photographs of three kinds of anti-counterfeiting CDs printed on a merchandise tag (left) and a banknote (right), taken in daylight (top) and under a UV excitation at 365 nm (bottom), respectively; (a) DETA-CDs in the UV curing polymer, (b)HP-PAMAM-CDs in the PVA film, (c) DETA-CDs in the NC film. The corresponding EDF and “three lines” spectra shown in the middle. The concentrations of these three samples were kept at 5 wt%.

Stability measurement of DETA-CDs in NC matrix at 5 wt%: (a) (c) EDF curves of the same sample measured before and after treatment, (b) three ways of treatment including exposure to UV light, sunlight, and heating at 60 °C, (d) comparison of “three-line” spectra: a printed dot on banknote before (solid line) and after (dash line) various treatments.
The values of fluorescence lifetime at different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Tunable excitation-dependent-fluorescence of carbon dots: Fingerprint curves for super anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei Normal University of Science and Technology, No. 360, Hebei Street, Qinhuangdao, Hebei province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Normal University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel concept of anti-counterfeiting is proposed, based on the special properties of excitation-dependent-fluorescence (EDF) and emission-dependent-excitation (EDE) of carbon dots (CDs) in polymer matrix. EDF and EDE behaviors can be controlled by adjusting the concentration of CDs or changing species of CDs and their corresponding polymer matrix. Meanwhile, multi-emission in EDF spectrum is observed, which is helpful to identification. Particularly, the curves in EDF or EDE spectrum as unique and sophisticated as human fingerprints are highly difficult to replicate, which promise practical applications of CDs in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

A novel concept of anti-counterfeiting is proposed, based on the unique excitation-dependent-fluorescence (EDF) of carbon dots (CDs). Multi-emission in EDF spectrum as unique and sophisticated as human fingerprints is highly difficult to replicate.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting is an increasing international problem that causes financial damages and posts security threats to customers, companies, and society. During the past decades, many kinds of anti-counterfeiting materials have been adopted to protect valuable documents such as banknotes, invoices, certificates and labels [1,2]. However, the current anti-counterfeiting materials are relatively well-known to the counterfeiters, and cannot effectively prevent the illegal replicating. Recently, researchers have put forward some excellent methods for advanced anti-counterfeiting, including triple-mode emission [3], magnetic response [4,5], AFM nanoxerography [6], and luminescence printing [7,8]. But these proposed methods have their own disadvantages like expensive equipment, tedious preparation, toxicity and harmful solvent. Consequently, the development of anti-counterfeiting materials and techniques is in high demand around the world.

Recent research on fluorescence (FL) of carbon dots (CDs) has become a new hot spot in material science [9,10]. Especially, a wide variety of nitrogen-contained organic compounds can be used as precursors to fabricate FL CDs by heat-treatment or hydrothermal method [11]. FL CDs exhibit many unique properties, including good photostability, low toxicity, special FL behavior, as well as facile and low-cost synthesis, which signifying many potential applications in optical devices, biomedical science, sensors, and detection. And the first to apply FL CDs in the field of anti-counterfeiting is Lin's group in 2016 [3], they demonstrated the special optical behavior of triple mode emission of CDs (that is, FL, up-conversion FL, and phosphorescence) could be utilized to fabricate advanced anti-counterfeiting products. Shortly after this work, a kinds of CDs-coating/rare-earth ions doped materials with dual FL modes of up-conversion and down-conversion was applied in anti-counterfeiting [12]. These pioneering works implied that FL CDs might be a promising material for anti-counterfeiting owing to their unique properties.

Encouraged by this, herein, we propose a new technique for super anti-counterfeiting based on the special property of excitation-dependent-fluorescence (EDF) of CDs. In order to achieve the goal, the behaviors of CDs in different polymer matrix are systematically explored. It is found that EDF can be easily tuned and precisely controlled by adjusting concentration of CDs. Meanwhile, EDF behaviors are also affected by the change of species of CDs precursor and polymer matrix. Moreover, the phenomena of multi-emission of CDs in polymer matrix are observed, benefiting the facile identification in anti-counterfeiting. Particularly, EDF spectra of CDs exhibit sophisticated curves, the combinations of these curves are as unique as human fingerprints, making it extremely difficult to imitate. Considering other merits of CDs, it is a plausible assumption that CDs are a kind of ideal materials for super anti-counterfeiting and can be applied in practical use on a large scale in the near future.

UV-curable epoxy acrylate oligomer was purchased from Guangdong Hengda New Materials Technology Co., Ltd. Nitrocellulose coating (NC) was bought from Shanghai Bauhinia Coating Co., Ltd. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, alcoholysis degree: 87.0–89.0%), diethylenetriamine (DETA), polyethyene polyamine (PEPA) and other reagents used in this work were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as received without further treatments or purification. Ultrapure water was used in all cases whenever water was addressed.

Hyperbranched poly (amido amine) (HP-PAMAM) was prepared by one-pot method reported before [18]. A 0.02 mol methanol solutions of ethylenediamine (1 × 10−2 mol L−1) was dropwise added into a 0.02 mol methyl acrylate. Then, the mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 48 h.

PEPA, DETA and HP-PAMAM were adopted as precursors to fabricate three kinds of CDs, respectively, using the heat-treatment method [11]. PEPA-CDs and DETA-CDs were synthesized by heating at 190 °C for 30 h and 6 h, respectively. HP-PAMAM-CDs were fabricated by heating at 70 °C for 14 days. The as-prepared products were dissolved in ethanol, and further purified by high speed centrifugation (8000 rpm, 3 min) to get rid of large precipitates. Then, the supernatant liquid was separated and filtered via a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained solution was put into a vacuum oven to thoroughly remove the rest of ethanol solvent.

Film samples of the obtained CDs products in different polymer matrix were prepared by the following method. For the CDs samples in PVA or NC matrix, a certain amount CDs were dispersed in PVA (6 wt%) aqueous solution or NC dispersion, and dropwise added onto a piece of quartz plate (25 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm) and dried it. For the CDs samples in UV polymer matrix, a certain amount CDs was dispersed in UV curing polymer, and dropwise added onto a piece of quartz plate (25 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm), then polymerized under a UV-lamp (395 nm) irradiation for 10 min. In order to achieve more stable samples, all prepared film samples were post-treated in oven at 60 °C for 1 h.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a SHIMADZU UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Excitation-emission matrices and fluorescence measurement were performed on a Hitachi F-7000, and the slit width of excitation and emission were fixed at 10 nm. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Tensor-27 spectrometer, and KBr pellets of powders were used in measurement. Measurements of the fluorescence lifetimes were performed with standard time-correlated single-photon counting method (FLS-920, Edinburgh Instruments), the exciting light was a portable diode laser with 320 nm (69 ps, 0.10 mW) laser beam, and fluorescence was detected at the emission maximum of the sample. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra of CDs were recorded on a Bruker Avance 600 Hz spectrometer using tetramethylsilane (TMS, δ = 0 ppm) as internal standard, and using the D2O as the solvent. The high resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) measurements were performed on a LTQ Orbitrap XL with positive-ion electrospray ionization (ESI) using methanol as the solvent. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were carried out with a Hitach H-7650 performing with an acceleration voltage of 80 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability test of CDs in polymer matrix
ce:section.ce:para: To check the stability to UV light, CDs films on quartz plates or samples on banknotes were irradiated by UV light at the optimal excitation wavelength emitted from a Xe lamp. The test of stability to sunlight was conducted in bright sunny days, CDs films on quartz plates were put outdoors in sunlight exposure. CDs samples were placed in an oven at 60 °C to check the stability at elevated temperatures. The sample of aging test was laid on table under ambient condition, covered with a dustproof quartz glass. For comparing purposes in this test, EDF spectra of all samples were measured before and after corresponding treatments.

The FT-IR spectra of PEPA-CDs, DETA-CDs and HP-PAMAM-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 a–c. The peaks at 3380 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1corresponds to deformation vibration of N–H [12]; the peaks at 2960 and 2855 cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of sp3 (-C-H) [9,14]; the peaks at 1485 cm−1 and 1327 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration C–N [28]. 1H NMR spectra of PEPA-CDs, DETA-CDs and HP-PAMAM-CDs have been recorded using 600 MHz spectrometer in D2O solvent and are shown in Fig. 1d–f. The appearance of signals in the range of 2.4–2.9 ppm can be ascribed to methylene groups connecting to alkyl or amine groups [29,30]. While, for the sample of HP-PAMAM-CDs, the differentiated signals in the range of 3.2–3.8 ppm possibly originate from methylene groups link to certain functional groups, such as carbonyl groups and amine oxides. Furthermore, the morphology of HP-PAMAM-CDs is characterized by TEM (Fig. S1), the TEM images clearly confirm that as-prepared CDs exhibit a relatively rounded and well-dispersed in the size range from 20 to 40 nm. The HRMS data of PEPA-CDs, DETA-CDs and HP-PAMAM-CDs provide some information on the aggregation of compounds (Fig. S2). Unfortunately, it is difficult to give a confident suggestion of the aggregates based on our present data.

It is known that the products of FL CDs are usually uncertain materials, which might be carbonaceous compounds formed by oxidation [24–26]. Therefore, up to now, hardly any precise composition and structure of FL CDs (prepared by heat-treatment or hydrothermal method) have been determined, although many techniques (such as, element analysis, electronic microscopy, nuclear magnetic resonance, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction) have been used in previously reported papers. Nevertheless, for the ultimate purpose of utilizing CDs as anti-counterfeiting materials, the great difficulty at present in characterization of CDs will not impede the manufacture of CDs and their applications in anti-counterfeiting field. Interestingly, the difficulty makes it almost impossible for counterfeiter to decode the molecular information of CDs, and, thus, unable to illegally duplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-emission in EDF and EDE spectra of PEPA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, PEPA-CDs in a PVA film exhibit emission-dependent-excitation (EDE, Fig. 2a, c, e) spectra, with unique fingerprint feature as EDF (Fig. 2b, d, f). It is notable that these EDE spectra also demonstrate multi-emission as that of EDF, with two excitation bands at about 365 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of these samples show a dominating absorption band at around 370 nm (dash line shown in Fig. 2a, c, e), which mirrored in the major excitation band in EDE spectra. As discussed in previous literatures, both multi-emission and multi-excitation are special properties for CDs, and can be ascribed to the polydispersity of CDs and various kinds of FL centers (attributed to different kinds of aggregates) [19–21]. In particular, this result suggests that anti-counterfeiting compounds of CDs can be double-checked by both EDF and EDE, making it more difficult to imitate their characteristic.

Based on the obtained results, we notice some excellent properties of CDs, making us more firmly believe in their potential as anti-counterfeiting materials. Firstly, EDF (or EDE) spectra can be precisely controlled and adjusted by varying the concentration of CDs, in other words, a tiny change in concentration can lead to a corresponding subtle change for the curves in EDF (or EDE) spectra. Secondly, although there are many different CD systems that exhibit similar optical behavior, EDF (or EDE) spectra of CDs possess significant features which are different from each other, depending on preparation method, precursor, polymer matrix, content and so on. In other words, the combination of these EDF (or EDE) curves is as unique and sophisticated as human fingerprint, each with their own characteristic, which making it extremely hard to imitate the original EDF. Meanwhile, the unique behavior of multi-emssion (or multi-excitation) peaks helps us to easily tell the differences among various systems of CDs. Last but not least, we confirm that the EDF (or EDE) data of the tested CDs samples are highly reproducible, if these samples are prepared in the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EDF spectra and FL lifetime of DETA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extend applications of CDs in anti-counterfeiting field, a series of samples of DETA-CDs in a UV curing polymer were investigated. As expected, the EDF behaviors and their dependence on the concentration of CDs are observed (Fig. 4 a–d). At a low content of DETA-CDs, the EDF has two major emission bands at about 410 nm and 440 nm, respectively. However, the band at shorter wavelength position (410 nm) increased with the increasing concentration of CDs. This change tendency was quite different from that of PEPA-CDs in PVA matrix as discussed in the above section. The result is possibly due to variation of the species of CDs and polymer matrix, because it has been proved that FL centers of CDs in solution are sensitive to surrounding environment [14,22,23]. On the other hand, in order to get more information on FL centers, FL lifetime decay profiles of DETA-CDs in UV curing polymer at different concentrations were collected at the emission wavelength of 400 nm and 435 nm (as shown in Fig. 4e and f). Both of them presented two lifetime species maintained at ca. 1.4 ns (τ1) and 5.5 ns (τ2), respectively (Table 1 ). Within the experimental errors, these two lifetime values can keep consistent for all samples. This result supports the deduction that there are two kinds of FL centers formed in the tested samples. Moreover, DETA-CDs in the UV curing polymer matrix show EDE spectra, and they also exhibit multi-excitation behavior, similar to that of PEPA-CDs in PVA matrix discussed above, with two bands at about 320 nm and 370 nm (Fig. 5 a–d). Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectra of these samples show a wide absorption band at around 350 nm (dash line shown in Fig. 5a–d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different systems of CDs in polymer matrix
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the diversity of EDF, we prepared various samples by utilizing different species of CDs and polymer matrix. Several representative spectra of samples at the same concentration of 5 wt% CDs are shown in Fig. 6 , including DETA-CDs or HP-PAMAM-CDs in PVA, DETA-CDs or PEPA-CDs in NC. All samples exhibit sophisticated fingerprint curves of EDF, and each of them differs from the others in terms of FL intensity, emission wavelength position and curve shape. Differences between these samples arise from arrangement and combination of CDs and polymer matrix (that is, the changes in FL centers and their environment). It is conceivable in the future, there are a lot of different kinds of CDs-polymer composites for us to adopt when manufacturing anti-counterfeiting materials, which makes it highly difficult for counterfeiters to find the real original compounds of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Double-layer film of different kinds of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to make CDs-polymer composites have more complex EDF spectra, we prepared double-layer samples consisting of two types of CDs film. As an example, it can be readily achieved by drop-casting a PEPA-CDs-PVA aqueous dispersion on a film of DETA-CDs in NC matrix. In a comparison to separate films, more sophisticated EDF spectra is observed (Fig. 7 ). It is obvious that the EDF fingerprint curves of the double-layer sample reflect the features of two separate films. This example clearly presents a facile way to enhance the security level of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application example in anti-counterfeiting of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the performance of CDs in anti-counterfeiting, by using three kinds of CDs-polymer composites, we printed patterns of circular spots on a banknote and square spots on the back of a merchandise tag, respectively (Fig. 8 right and left images). The printed CDs materials are nearly colorless, transparent and invisible in daylight, while, emitting bright blue-green light under a UV lamp (365 nm). The differences of the printed images are difficult to tell from FL appearance by naked eyes, however, it is easy to distinguish one image from another when checking the fingerprint EDF curves. Particularly, there is no doubt that the works of authentication in these cases are faithful and accurate due to the distinguished characteristics of EDF spectra. Meanwhile, in a practical application, we do not need to monitor all curves of EDF, unless it is necessary for a higher level identification. We confirmed that three typical curves were usually enough to verify the authenticity (Fig. 8a, b, c), which was a more facile way to identify in ordinary work (“three-line” spectra). On the other hand, compared with some new techniques for advanced anti-counterfeiting (expensive and sophisticated equipment required therein), the only instrument used in our method is FL spectrophotometer, which is relatively inexpensive and reliable. By the way, if FL spectrophotometer equipped with a set of fiber-optical accessory, a rapid examination or small-area analysis in anti-counterfeiting works will be easily developed.

Stability is a very important property for anti-counterfeiting materials in practical applications. Hence, we studied this issue using prepared DETA-CDs in NC matrix. Experimental verification was conducted in three ways, including exposure to UV light (Xe lamp), sunlight, and heating at 60 °C. It is observed that the intensity of the tested sample keeps almost unchanged in the testing process of these three ways (Fig. 9 b), meanwhile, the characteristics of fingerprint curves are identical before and after treatment (Fig. 9a, c). Similar result is also achieved for another sample (PEPA-CDs in PVA film), as shown in Fig. S4. These preliminary data demonstrate that the as-prepared CDs samples have reliable stability under common conditions. In particular, after a series of consecutive tests including UV light, sunlight and heating as well as simulating daily use, EDF curves of CDs materials printed on a banknote do not vary, namely, we can obtain the almost same “three-line” spectra measured before and after consecutive tests (Fig. 9d). In addition, there is no obvious difference in EDF spectra between the two measurements of a sample before and after three months (Fig. S5). The above results are consistent with the statements about the stability of CDs in many previous studies.

In summary, the special EDF properties of FL CDs in polymer matrix promise an attractive material for super anti-counterfeiting, because EDF curves as unique and sophisticated as human fingerprints are highly difficult to replicate. Importantly, the EDF behaviors of CDs can be controlled and tuned by adjusting the concentration. And the multi-emission phenomenon in the process of regulating concentration provides remarkable characteristics for anti-counterfeiting identification. Furthermore, our results verify that numerous different anti-counterfeiting compounds can be fabricated by varying species of CDs and polymer matrix. In particular, the difficulty in characterization of CDs make it almost impossible for counterfeiter to decode the molecular information of CDs. Together with other unique properties of FL CDs including good photostability, low toxicity and low-cost synthesis, our works present a novel authentication concept for super anti-counterfeiting, which can be used in real applications on a large scale.
Ke Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-Original Draft, Data Curation.
Rui Qi: Software, Methodology, Shuai Lan: Resources, Formal analysis, Supervision.
Hongyang Wang: Software, Methodology.
Xuefang Zheng, Resources, Supervision, Chun Liu: Project administration, Supervision.
Dandan Jia: Resources, Supervision, Lei Cao: Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision, Formal analysis.
Dongjun Wang: Data Curation, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-ReviewEditing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Foundation (2018005) from the Center of Analysis and Measurement, the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation (2019YB003) of Hebei Normal Univeristy of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.108106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent functional material based approaches to prevent and detect counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yoon B, Lee J, Park I S, et al. Recent functional material based approaches to prevent and detect counterfeiting. J. Mater. Chem. C 2013; 1: 2388-2403.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical nanomaterials and enabling technologies for high‐security‐level anticounterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ren W, Lin G, Clarke C, et al. Optical Nanomaterials and Enabling Technologies for High-Security-Level Anticounterfeiting. Adv. Mater 2019; 1901430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triple‐mode emission of carbon dots: applications for advanced anti‐counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang K, Zhang L, Lu J, et al. Triple-Mode Emission of Carbon Dots: Applications for Advanced Anti-Counterfeiting. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed 2016; 55: 7231-7235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetically responsive photonic watermarks on banknotes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu H, Zhong H, Chen C, et al. Magnetically responsive photonic watermarks on banknotes. J. Mater. Chem. C 2014; 2: 3695-3702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photonic anti-counterfeiting using structural colors derived from magnetic-responsive photonic crystals with double photonic bandgap heterostructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu H, Chen Q W, Tang J, et al. Photonic anti-counterfeiting using structural colors derived from magnetic-responsive photonic crystals with double photonic bandgap heterostructures. J. Mater. Chem. C 2012; 22: 11048-11053.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3D assembly of upconverting NaYF4 nanocrystals by AFM nanoxerography: creation of anti-counterfeiting microtags

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sangeetha N M, Moutet P, Lagarde D, et al. 3D assembly of upconverting NaYF4 nanocrystals by AFM nanoxerography: creation of anti-counterfeiting microtags. Nanoscale 2013; 5: 9587-9592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inkjet printing of upconversion nanoparticles for anti-counterfeit applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: You M, Zhong J, Hong Y, et al. Inkjet printing of upconversion nanoparticles for anti-counterfeit applications. Nanoscale 2015; 7: 4423-4431.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new anti‐counterfeiting feature relying on invisible luminescent full color images printed with lanthanide‐based inks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Andres J, Hersch R D, Moser J E, et al. A new anti-counterfeiting feature relying on invisible luminescent full color images printed with lanthanide-based inks. Adv. Funct. Mater 2014; 24: 5029-5036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li H, Kang Z, Liu Y, et al. Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications. J. Mater. Chem 2012; 22: 24230-24253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das R, Bandyopadhyay R, Pramanik P. Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: A review. Materials Today Chemistry 2018; 8: 96-109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uncovering a broad class of fluorescent amine-containing compounds by heat treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jia D, Cao L, Wang D, et al. Uncovering a broad class of fluorescent amine-containing compounds by heat treatment. Chem. Commun 2014; 50: 11488-11491.
M. X. Li, W. J. Yao, J. Liu, Q. Y. Tian, L. Liu, J. Ding, Q. W. Xue, Q. Lu, & W. Wu, J. Mater. Chem. C 2017; 5: 6512-6520.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sharma A, Gadly T, Gupta A, et al. Origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in carbon nanodots. J. Phys. Chem. Lett 2016; 7: 3695-3702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyethylenimine-derived fluorescent nonconjugated polymer dots with reversible dual-signal pH response and logic gate operation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu S G, Liu T, Li N, et al. Polyethylenimine-derived fluorescent nonconjugated polymer dots with reversible dual-signal pH response and logic gate operation. J. Phys. Chem. C 2017; 121: 12, 6874-6883.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non‐Conjugated polymer dots with crosslink‐enhanced emission in the absence of fluorophore units

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu S, Song Y, Shao J, et al. Non-Conjugated Polymer Dots with Crosslink-Enhanced Emission in the Absence of Fluorophore Units. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed 2015; 54: 14626-14637.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Concentration-induced multi-colored emissions in carbon dots: origination from triple fluorescent centers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen Y, Lian H, Wei Y, et al. Concentration-induced multi-colored emissions in carbon dots: origination from triple fluorescent centers. Nanoscale 2018; 10: 6734-6743.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self‐quenching‐resistant carbon‐dot powder with tunable solid‐state fluorescence and construction of dual‐fluorescence morphologies for white light‐emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen Y, Zheng M, Xiao Y, et al. A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission. Adv. Mater 2016; 28: 312-318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Significant influence of distance between amine groups on intrinsic fluorescence properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao L, Jia D, Wang S, et al. Significant Influence of Distance between Amine Groups on Intrinsic Fluorescence Properties. Chem. Lett 2014; 43: 246-248.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbonized polymer dots: a brand new perspective to recognize luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tao S, Feng T, Zheng C, et al. Carbonized Polymer Dots: A Brand New Perspective to Recognize Luminescent Carbon-Based Nanomaterials. J. Phys. Chem. Lett 2019; 10: 5182-5188.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) in non-conjugated polymer dots: from the photoluminescence mechanism to the cellular uptake mechanism and internalization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu S, Wang L, Zhou N, et al. The crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) in non-conjugated polymer dots: from the photoluminescence mechanism to the cellular uptake mechanism and internalization. Chem. Commun 2014; 50: 13845-13848.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao E, Lam J W Y, Meng L, et al. Poly [(maleic anhydride)-alt-(vinyl acetate)]: A pure oxygenic nonconjugated macromolecule with strong light emission and solvatochromic effect. Macromolecules 2015; 48: 64-71.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sciortino A, Marino E, Dam B, et al. Solvatochromism unravels the emission mechanism of carbon nanodots. J. Phys. Chem. Lett 2016; 7: 3419-3423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent dependence of the intrinsic fluorescence from amine-containing compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liang H, Jia D, Liu H, et al. Solvent dependence of the intrinsic fluorescence from amine-containing compounds. Chem. Lett 2015; 44: 548-550.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The polymeric characteristics and photoluminescence mechanism in polymer carbon dots: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tao S, Zhu S, Feng T, et al. The polymeric characteristics and photoluminescence mechanism in polymer carbon dots: A review. Materials Today Chemistry 2017; 6: 13-25.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-traditional intrinsic luminescence: inexplicable blue fluorescence observed for dendrimers, macromolecules and small molecular structures lacking traditional/conventional luminophores

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tomalia D A, Klajnert-Maculewicz B, Johnson K A M, et al. Non-traditional intrinsic luminescence: inexplicable blue fluorescence observed for dendrimers, macromolecules and small molecular structures lacking traditional/conventional luminophores. Progress in Polymer Science 2019; 90: 35-117.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding H, Wei J S, Zhong N, et al. Highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots with gram-scale yield for bioimaging. Langmuir 2017; 33: 12635-12642.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang H J, Hou W Y, Yu T T, et al. Facile microwave synthesis of carbon dots powder with enhanced solid-state fluorescence and its applications in rapid fingerprints detection and white-light-emitting diodes. Dyes and Pigments 2019; 107623.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang H, Sun C, Chen X, et al. Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots. Nanoscale 2017; 9: 1909-1915.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang W, Shi L, Liu Y, et al. Supramolecular interactions via hydrogen bonding contributing to citric-acid derived carbon dots with high quantum yield and sensitive photoluminescence. RSC advances 2017; 7: 20345-20353.
